content.text: An Azure Geography is a discrete market boundary that defines which Azure regions share the same data residency and compliance constraints. Where a region is the physical deployment unit (a set of datacentres in one city), a geography is the regulatory unit — the boundary within which GRS replication stays, within which Microsoft commits to keeping data at rest, and within which regional failovers occur.

The geography concept exists because data sovereignty laws — GDPR in the EU, the UK GDPR, the Australian Privacy Act, China's PIPL, US federal data residency requirements — mandate that certain categories of data never cross national or jurisdictional borders. Azure's geography structure gives customers a simple guarantee: deploy within a geography, enable geo-redundant storage, and your data stays within that geography's legal boundary during both normal operation and regional failover.

A Geography is a contractual and regulatory boundary, not just a physical one. Microsoft's Data Protection Addendum (DPA) commits that data stored in a geography's regions remains within that geography during replication and disaster recovery — this is a legally binding commitment, not a configuration option.

attrs.alt: Azure Geography hierarchy for Europe: multiple regions (North Europe, West Europe, UK South, Germany WC) with Availability Zones inside a GDPR data residency boundary

content.text: Each geography contains multiple regions, and each region contains multiple Availability Zones. All replication within a geography — whether GRS storage, SQL Database geo-replication, or Cosmos DB multi-region writes — stays within the geography boundary by default.

The US geography is one of the largest, containing more Azure regions than any other: East US, East US 2, West US, West US 2, West US 3, Central US, North Central US, South Central US, West Central US. It is suitable for most commercial US workloads. Government workloads with federal compliance requirements (FedRAMP, IL4, IL5, CJIS) use the separate Azure Government geography, which is physically isolated from commercial Azure.

The Europe geography encompasses multiple countries that share GDPR data protection requirements: Ireland (North Europe), Netherlands (West Europe), United Kingdom (UK South, UK West), Germany (Germany West Central, Germany North), France (France Central, France South), Switzerland (Switzerland North, Switzerland West), Norway (Norway East, Norway West), Sweden (Sweden Central, Sweden South), and Poland (Poland Central). All GRS replication within this geography stays within the EEA or UK — appropriate for GDPR-governed data.

Asia Pacific spans Singapore (Southeast Asia), Hong Kong (East Asia), Japan (Japan East, Japan West), South Korea (Korea Central, Korea South), Australia (Australia East, Australia Southeast), and India (India Central, India West, India South). Each sub-geography within Asia Pacific has its own data residency considerations — Australian data residency requires deployment to Australian regions, not to the broader Asia Pacific geography.
Sovereign geographies are physically and logically isolated from the commercial Azure network:

Separate Azure fabric, operated by US persons with government clearances. Regions: USGov Virginia, USGov Texas, USGov Arizona, USDoD Central, USDoD East. Complies with FedRAMP High, DoD CC SRG IL4/IL5, ITAR, and CJIS. Cannot be accessed from a commercial Azure subscription.

Operated by 21Vianet under a separate Azure licence. Complies with China's Multi-Level Protection Scheme (MLPS 2.0) and PIPL. Regions: China East, China East 2, China East 3, China North, China North 2, China North 3. Requires a Chinese legal entity and 21Vianet contract — not accessible via standard Azure accounts.
East US, West US 2, Central US, South Central US
North Europe, West Europe, UK South, Germany WC, France Central, Switzerland North, Norway East, Sweden Central
Southeast Asia, East Asia, Japan East, Korea Central
India Central, India West, India South
UAE North, UAE Central, Qatar Central
South Africa North, South Africa West
USGov Virginia, USGov Texas, USGov Arizona, USDoD Central
Data Residency Enforcement via Azure Policy

Declaring a geography in documentation is not sufficient for compliance — you must enforce it technically. Azure Policy allows you to restrict resource deployment to a set of approved regions, preventing accidental deployment of regulated data to an out-of-scope region.

# Create a policy that restricts deployment to EU regions only (GDPR boundary)
# Built-in policy: "Allowed locations" (policy ID: e56962a6-4747-49cd-b67b-bf8b01975c4c)

az policy assignment create \
  --name "restrict-to-eu-regions" \
  --display-name "GDPR: Restrict resources to EU geography" \
  --policy "e56962a6-4747-49cd-b67b-bf8b01975c4c" \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subscription-id> \
  --params '{
    "listOfAllowedLocations": {
      "value": [
        "northeurope",
        "westeurope",
        "uksouth",
        "ukwest",
        "germanywestcentral",
        "germanynorth",
        "francecentral",
        "francesouth",
        "switzerlandnorth",
        "switzerlandwest",
        "norwayeast",
        "norwaywest",
        "swedencentral",
        "swedensouth",
        "polandcentral"
      ]
    }
  }'

# Verify the assignment is active
az policy assignment show \
  --name "restrict-to-eu-regions" \
  --query "{Name:name, Scope:scope, EnforcementMode:enforcementMode}" \
  --output json

The Allowed Locations policy uses deny enforcement — any attempt to deploy a resource outside the listed regions fails immediately at the ARM layer, before any data is written. This policy should be applied at the management group level so it covers all subscriptions in the GDPR scope.

Geo-Redundant Storage (GRS) always replicates to the paired region within the same geography. This means that enabling GRS is inherently geography-compliant — you cannot accidentally configure GRS to replicate to a region in a different geography.

# Create a storage account in North Europe (Ireland) with GZRS
# GRS secondary is automatically West Europe (Netherlands) — within EU geography
az storage account create \
  --name eucompliancestorage \
  --resource-group eu-prod-rg \
  --location northeurope \
  --sku Standard_GZRS \
  --kind StorageV2 \
  --allow-blob-public-access false \
  --min-tls-version TLS1_2

# Confirm secondary location (must be within EU geography)
az storage account show \
  --name eucompliancestorage \
  --resource-group eu-prod-rg \
  --query "{Primary:primaryLocation, Secondary:secondaryLocation, SKU:sku.name}" \
  --output json
# Expected output: primary=northeurope, secondary=westeurope — both within EU geography

For workloads where you need to restrict data to a specific sub-region within a geography (e.g., data must stay in Germany even though the EU geography also includes Ireland and Netherlands), use ZRS (zone-redundant within one region) rather than GRS, and ensure no cross-region replication is configured.

Azure Government is a sovereign geography for US federal, state, local, and tribal government agencies, and their approved contractors. It is physically and logically separated from commercial Azure — a US Government subscription cannot access commercial Azure resources and vice versa.

# Authenticate to Azure Government cloud (separate ARM endpoint)
az cloud set --name AzureUSGovernment

az login \
  --tenant <government-tenant-id> \
  --username <gov-user@agency.gov>

# List available regions in Azure Government
az account list-locations \
  --query "[].{Name:name, DisplayName:displayName}" \
  --output table
# Returns: usgovvirginia, usgovtexas, usgovarizona, usdodcentral, usdodeast

# Deploy a resource in Azure Government
az group create \
  --name agency-prod-rg \
  --location usgovvirginia

az storage account create \
  --name agencyprodstg \
  --resource-group agency-prod-rg \
  --location usgovvirginia \
  --sku Standard_GRS \
  --kind StorageV2

# Return to commercial Azure
az cloud set --name AzureCloud

Azure China (21Vianet) is operated under a unique legal structure: Microsoft licences the technology to 21Vianet, a Chinese company that operates the datacentres and provides the services. This means Microsoft has no operational control over Azure China — 21Vianet does. Data never leaves mainland China under this model.
Requires a Chinese legal entity (ICP filing), a 21Vianet account, and a Chinese bank account for billing. Cannot be accessed with a standard Microsoft account.

Approximately 70–80% of Azure services are available, some with a time lag relative to commercial Azure. Not all services are identical — Azure OpenAI is available with a separate model set and use restrictions.
management.chinacloudapi.cn (not management.azure.com). The CLI uses az cloud set --name AzureChinaCloud.
Azure China is certified under China's Multi-Level Protection Scheme at levels 1–4, satisfying the security classification requirements for Chinese government and critical infrastructure workloads.
Geography vs. Region vs. Availability Zone: When to Use Each
The three levels of Azure's geographic hierarchy serve different purposes in resilience and compliance planning:
Geo-redundant failover; data deployed per workload
All DCs in a city/region fail (natural disaster, regional outage)
Data residency; compliance boundary for GRS, law enforcement

A well-designed architecture uses all three levels: zone-redundant deployments protect against single-DC failures; region pairs protect against regional disasters; and geography selection enforces data residency. Choosing a geography determines which region pairs are available; choosing a region determines which AZs are available; enabling zone redundancy in services uses those AZs.
GDPR and the EU Geography: What Microsoft Guarantees

GDPR Article 44 prohibits transferring personal data to third countries unless adequate protections are in place. Deploying to Azure's EU geography — and enabling GRS to replicate only within the EU geography — satisfies the data transfer requirements because the data never leaves EU/EEA jurisdiction.

Microsoft's Data Processing Addendum (DPA) includes a specific commitment called the 'EU Data Boundary': personal data processed by Microsoft's core online services (Azure, Dynamics, Microsoft 365) for EU customers is stored and processed within the EU. This commitment applies automatically when you deploy to EU geography regions — you do not need to request it separately.

# Verify that a resource group and its resources are within EU geography
az resource list \
  --resource-group eu-prod-rg \
  --query "[].{Name:name, Type:type, Location:location}" \
  --output table

# Confirm storage account secondary is in EU (West Europe for North Europe primary)
az storage account show \
  --name eucompliancestorage \
  --resource-group eu-prod-rg \
  --query "{Primary:primaryLocation, Secondary:secondaryLocation, SKU:sku.name}" \
  --output json

# List all resources across subscriptions that are OUTSIDE EU geography
# (compliance audit: identify out-of-geography resources)
EU_REGIONS="northeurope westeurope uksouth ukwest germanywestcentral germanynorth francecentral francesouth switzerlandnorth norwayeast swedencentral polandcentral"

az resource list \
  --query "[?!contains('$EU_REGIONS', location)].{Name:name, Location:location, Type:type}" \
  --output table 2>/dev/null | head -50

The compliance audit query above is a starting point — production compliance scanning should use Azure Policy compliance reports, which cover all subscriptions in a management group hierarchy and handle resource types that the CLI resource list may not surface (e.g., Azure AD, management group policy assignments themselves).

Some organisations operate across multiple geographies — a European headquarters with US and APAC subsidiaries, for example. Multi-geography architectures require careful data classification to determine which data can flow between geographies and which must stay within a geography boundary.

Classify data by sensitivity: PII governed by GDPR, healthcare data governed by HIPAA, financial data governed by PCI DSS, and public/non-regulated data each have different cross-geography transfer rules.
For PII: implement Data Subject Access Request (DSAR) automation that queries only the regions in the customer's geography. A UK customer's PII queried from a US-region datastore violates UK GDPR.

For application configuration and metadata: these are typically non-personal and can cross geographies. Centralise config in a single region (e.g., East US) and distribute to regional app deployments.

For logs and monitoring telemetry: Application Insights and Log Analytics workspaces store telemetry. Create a workspace per geography for personal telemetry data; a global workspace for infrastructure metrics only.
Use Azure Purview (Microsoft Purview) data map to classify data automatically across regions and flag cross-geography flows that violate residency rules.

# Create separate Log Analytics workspaces per geography for GDPR compliance
# EU workspace for EU region telemetry
az monitor log-analytics workspace create \
  --workspace-name la-eu-prod \
  --resource-group eu-prod-rg \
  --location northeurope \
  --retention-time 90

# US workspace for US region telemetry
az monitor log-analytics workspace create \
  --workspace-name la-us-prod \
  --resource-group us-prod-rg \
  --location eastus \
  --retention-time 90

# Configure Application Insights to use EU workspace for EU deployments
az monitor app-insights component create \
  --app eu-app-insights \
  --location northeurope \
  --resource-group eu-prod-rg \
  --workspace /subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/eu-prod-rg/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/la-eu-prod

# Configure US app to use US workspace
az monitor app-insights component create \
  --app us-app-insights \
  --location eastus \
  --resource-group us-prod-rg \
  --workspace /subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/us-prod-rg/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/la-us-prod

Infrastructure automation and cost management pipelines often need to determine whether a resource is in a specific geography, or to enumerate all regions in a geography to apply consistent configuration.

# Python: determine geography for a given Azure region name
# Uses the azure-mgmt-resource SDK to fetch location metadata

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource import SubscriptionClient

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = SubscriptionClient(credential)
subscription_id = "<your-subscription-id>"

locations = list(client.subscriptions.list_locations(subscription_id))

# Build a region -> geography mapping from location metadata
# Azure API exposes 'geographyGroup' in location metadata
region_geography = {}
for loc in locations:
    if loc.metadata:
        geo_group = getattr(loc.metadata, 'geography_group', None)
        region_geography[loc.name] = {
            "displayName": loc.display_name,
            "geography": geo_group,
            "regionType": getattr(loc.metadata, 'region_type', None),
            "pairedRegion": loc.metadata.paired_region[0].name if loc.metadata.paired_region else None
        }

# Find all EU geography regions
eu_regions = {k: v for k, v in region_geography.items() if v["geography"] == "Europe"}
print(f"EU geography regions ({len(eu_regions)}):")
for name, info in sorted(eu_regions.items()):
    print(f"  {name}: {info['displayName']} (pair: {info['pairedRegion']})")

# Check if a specific region is in the EU geography
target_region = "northeurope"
geo = region_geography.get(target_region, {}).get("geography", "Unknown")
print(f"\n{target_region} geography: {geo}")
print(f"GDPR compliant: {geo == 'Europe'}")

# CLI: list all regions grouped by geography
az account list-locations \
  --query "[?metadata.regionType=='Physical'].{Name:name, Geography:metadata.geographyGroup, Physical:displayName}" \
  --output table | sort -k2

# Filter for Europe only
az account list-locations \
  --query "[?metadata.geographyGroup=='Europe' && metadata.regionType=='Physical'].{Name:name, Display:displayName, Paired:metadata.pairedRegion[0].name}" \
  --output table

Each Azure geography is certified for the regulatory frameworks relevant to its jurisdiction. Understanding which certifications apply to your target geography is part of the pre-deployment compliance review:
ISO 27001, ISO 27701, SOC 1/2/3, GDPR (EU Data Boundary), ENS (Spain), DORA (EU financial), BSI C5 (Germany)
ISO 27001, SOC 2, Cyber Essentials Plus, NHS DSPT, UK NCSC Cloud Security Principles
SOC 1/2/3, ISO 27001, FedRAMP Moderate (commercial), HIPAA, PCI DSS, StateRAMP
FedRAMP High, DoD CC SRG IL2/IL4/IL5, CJIS, ITAR, IRS 1075, NIST 800-171
IRAP (Protected), ISO 27001, SOC 1/2, ASD Essential Eight, Australian Privacy Act
FISC, ISO 27001, My Number Act (JPMC), SOC 2
MLPS Level 1–4, DJCP (Classified Protection), MIIT ICP licence required

Compliance certification does not mean automatic compliance — it means the Azure platform meets the certification requirements. You are responsible for configuring your workloads to meet your own compliance obligations. Azure's Compliance Manager (in the Microsoft Purview portal) provides an assessment dashboard with control-by-control guidance for your specific regulatory framework.
Common Mistakes When Thinking About Geographies

Confusing geography with region: 'Europe' is a geography; 'North Europe' is a region. An Azure Policy must list specific region names (northeurope, westeurope, uksouth...) — not the geography name 'Europe'.

Assuming GRS can cross geographies: GRS always replicates to the paired region within the same geography. There is no GRS option that replicates to a region in a different geography. If your primary is North Europe, the GRS secondary is West Europe — not East US.

Confusing UK with EU geography post-Brexit: since January 2021, the UK is a separate geography from the EU. If you need both EU and UK data residency (e.g., a company with EU and UK subsidiaries), you need separate deployments — UK data must stay in UK South / UK West; EU data must stay in EU geography regions. A single North Europe deployment does not satisfy both.

Using RA-GRS endpoint for writes: the secondary endpoint of a Read-Access GRS storage account is read-only under normal operation. Applications that attempt writes to the secondary endpoint receive a 409 error. Writes only go to the primary endpoint.

Not testing GDPR compliance after a non-geography deployment: if a developer deploys a test resource in East US and copies production EU data there for debugging, that is a GDPR data transfer violation — even if temporary. Data classification and access controls must prevent this.
Geography Selection for New Azure Deployments: A Decision Process

Choosing the wrong geography for a new deployment is costly to fix — migrating resources between regions (and geographies) typically requires redeploying from scratch. Work through these decision points before provisioning the first resource:
Identify your data subjects: who are the individuals whose data you process? If they are EU residents, GDPR applies regardless of where your company is headquartered — you need EU geography.
Identify applicable regulations: GDPR (EU), UK GDPR, Australian Privacy Act, LGPD (Brazil), PIPL (China), PDPB (India), APPI (Japan). Each maps to a specific geography or pair of geographies.

Determine if sovereign cloud is required: US federal agencies and their contractors must assess whether FedRAMP High (Azure Government) is required. Standard commercial Azure is FedRAMP Moderate but not High.

Check service availability: confirm every service in your architecture is available in the required geography regions. If a critical service is only in US regions, you must either accept data transfer mechanisms (Standard Contractual Clauses) or find an alternative service.

Select primary and DR regions within the geography: pick the region pair within your target geography. For EU: North Europe/West Europe or UK South/UK West. For US: East US/West US 2 or East US 2/Central US.

Document the geography selection in your architecture decision record (ADR): include the regulatory basis, the specific regions chosen, the replication tier (GRS/GZRS/ZRS), and any exceptions with approved data transfer mechanisms.

# Architecture validation script: confirm all resources in a subscription are in approved geography
# Run this as part of a CI/CD compliance gate

APPROVED_REGIONS="northeurope westeurope uksouth ukwest germanywestcentral germanynorth francecentral francesouth switzerlandnorth switzerlandwest norwayeast swedencentral polandcentral"

echo "=== Geography Compliance Check ==="
echo "Approved regions: EU geography (GDPR)"
echo ""

# Get all resources and check their region
NON_COMPLIANT=$(az resource list   --query "[].{Name:name, Location:location, Type:type}"   --output json | python3 -c "
import json, sys
approved = set('$APPROVED_REGIONS'.split())
resources = json.load(sys.stdin)
non_compliant = [r for r in resources if r['Location'] not in approved and r['Location'] not in ('global', '')]
print(json.dumps(non_compliant, indent=2))
")

COUNT=$(echo "$NON_COMPLIANT" | python3 -c "import json,sys; print(len(json.load(sys.stdin)))")

if [ "$COUNT" -eq 0 ]; then
  echo "PASS: All $COUNT resources are within EU geography"
else
  echo "FAIL: $COUNT resource(s) found outside EU geography:"
  echo "$NON_COMPLIANT"
  exit 1
fi

Running this script in a CI/CD pipeline as a compliance gate ensures that any deployment to a non-EU region is caught before it reaches production. Combine it with the Azure Policy 'Allowed Locations' deny assignment for defence in depth — policy prevents the deployment; the script provides audit evidence.

Some Azure resources report their location as 'global' rather than a specific region — Azure Active Directory, Azure DNS, Azure Front Door, Traffic Manager, and Azure CDN profiles are examples. Global resources are geographically distributed by design and do not store customer data in a single geography.

Directory data is replicated globally across Microsoft datacentres. The specific geography where tenant data is stored depends on the country selected during tenant creation — EU-created tenants store data in EU datacentres. Check the 'Where is your data located?' page in the Azure Admin portal.
CDN and WAF configuration is distributed globally; access logs are stored in a Log Analytics workspace that you control (and should place in the appropriate geography).
DNS-based load balancer with globally distributed resolvers; no customer data stored — only DNS records and health probe configuration.
Zone data is replicated globally for DNS resolution latency, but DNS records themselves are not personal data for most workloads.

For compliance purposes, 'global' resources require a separate assessment: identify what data (if any) each global resource stores, determine the geography of that data (check Microsoft's per-service data location documentation), and confirm it satisfies your regulatory requirements.

The geography boundary governs where your data at rest is stored and processed. It does not prevent users in other countries from accessing your application over the internet — geography is a data location control, not an access control.

An Azure Geography is the data residency and compliance boundary that groups Azure regions into legally coherent units. Data stored in EU geography regions stays within the EEA; data in Australian regions stays in Australia; sovereign geographies (Azure Government, Azure China) are physically isolated from commercial Azure. GRS replication is geography-bounded by design. Compliance enforcement requires both the correct region selection and an Azure Policy Allowed Locations assignment to prevent accidental out-of-geography deployments. The three-level hierarchy — Geography → Region → Availability Zone — maps directly to three levels of resilience concern: regulatory jurisdiction, regional disaster, and single-datacentre failure.
